kept.text:
@TheRayMyers Your inexperience is causing you to ask the wrong questions.

With $SOFI, it’s not about “are they growing?” It’s about “are they lending well?”

I almost never see bulls ask that. It’s always revenue up, EBITDA up, EPS up, and then shock when the stock doesn’t move or goes down. But this isn’t $AAPL, it’s a lending business. You have to look at it completely differently, mainly the balance sheet. 

Do they have a real funding advantage and are deposits actually sticky? Is underwriting great or are they just pushing volume to show growth? What do returns look like after losses? Are they building a relationship with the customer and cross selling higher margin products, or just originating loans and moving on? 

Because lending can look amazing on the way up. You can grow really fast and everything looks amazing, numbers look strong, story sounds great. 

And then the cycle turns and the market isn’t dumb. It’s asking a different question. Not what it looks like today, but what it looks like when things get harder when they cycle turns. 

This business and all lending / credit businesses as a whole are far more difficult and complex than what initially meets the eye… 

🌹

quoted_tweet.text:
@kalterkoney I’m a commentator not an analyst. There are def better analysts. Mostly intend to understand the industry really well because I cover the other banks. 

As relates to next 5 year, if you look at banking in general you still have a pretty fragmented market. And that is just in the US. My personal opinion is that $sofi, given size won’t freak other countries out so much with its stated ambition/interest of buying an overseas player to simplify entry into banking. 

Very few within banking ever care about the TTS biz. It’s boring but it’s awesome, cash flowing, stable and crazy strategic for the United States. This biz produced 17% growth for $bk and even in normal times is a single digit grower. But it hasn’t had a legit disrupter in a long time. Also, it necessarily forms a stable capital base. 

I think it grows that biz while it also just takes share in consumer banking. As it fortifies consumer banking I think it just naturally builds the asset management piece for those that want passive allocation (so not branch office, no financial planner). 

The wild card upside areas are whatever it decides to do in biz banking and investment banking. If he goes the DPO route then you start with investment banking and grow backwards into business banking. 

I used to hate DPO’s but in fairness the industry/world is changing. 

If instead they go the other route and grow biz banking via stablecoin (so cheaper faster overseas/international payments) that’s also awesome. 

I could care less about the fintech because tts & banking have great margins, are already the tech they have/are building.  And don’t take nearly as much capex to stay “relevant” in a rapidly changing tech landscape. 

But again, 1 girl’s Friday opinion shooting from the hip having never been asked this question I might change my mind tomorrow. 

Oh… and in all scenarios $sofi is at least 2x the size in 5years on the low end of those scenarios regardless of whether the market gives it credit. 

Day traders, short sellers and momentum traders are A-holes though.  They make sh*t up and the industry itself is hard to understand . So,  it can go down before up. So again, not advice. 

Happy Friday 😊🙃😊🙃

kept.text:
$SOFI at $16 is getting interesting. 

The company just posted 41% revenue growth, accelerating from Q4, driven by $690M in lending net interest income.

The stock is down because $SOFI beat Q1 but didn’t raise the full-year guide.

But here’s the important part:

Noto said the original outlook assumed two rate cuts… and now they don’t expect any rate cuts this year.

Yet they still maintained the same guidance.

That tells me the business is executing better than expected internally, because the macro assumptions got worse and the guide didn’t move lower.

The market sees “no raise.”

I see a company growing 41%, expanding the platform, and holding guidance despite losing the rate-cut tailwind.

People loved $SOFI at $30+ with weaker fundamentals.

At $16, with the business much stronger, it’s hard not to pay attention.

profile_bio.description: 23 | Finance student | Long-term growth investor | Focused on $HIMS, $SOFI, $OSCR, $NBIS, $ZETA, $DUOL & more emerging winners 🕷️ NFA

kept.text:
$SOFI... fan of @insiderinvests, but let's clarify some things since I can see the bears going bonkers in the comments section.

1) Credit Quality Difference from 2021 to 2026. This is a 2021-B Trust, meaning this bond was done in 2021.  That means the defaults were done at that point in history when rates were crazy low. The 2.02% default rate is a nothing burger, b/c default rates - even with high credit quality have increased since 2021. How high have they gotten... 

IDK and someone can just look that up. Likely closer to 2.5-2.75% on specifically this credit quality given what some of the banks reported this quarter, possibly higher. But without that piece, the 2.02% is a bit meaningless, other than it's pretty low.

2) What might then be the risks? This bond ends in 2047. Notice the collateral that remains. This speaks to the pre-payment risk situation that has been openly and frequently discussed. 

Macro Comment: In a declining rate environment, you'd expect a certain level of refi's on this. That the prepayment period is a lot longer than when this was originally written is a real thing. Before the crazy rate increases, the prepayment rates used were about 2 years shorter than what's been going on at this point. 

Clearly stated though: If you get a refi boom all of this goes away immediately. If you don't, then you're just going to have this come in - in the recent period - slightly higher, but (given the entire term of the loan) overall still come in with the original assumptions of default.

3) The number on defaults that actually matters is the Cumulative Default rate of 1.79%. That will take out the weird-looking effect of pre-payment changes in collateral balance, the duration component, etc. And here's the thing 1.79% for consumer credit is crazy low. 

Anywho, hope that helps.

kept.text:
Holy sh*t

This guy single-handedly pumped $SoFi today https://t.co/2dLoxeOHLA

$SOFI deposit growth might be one of the most underrated parts of the entire thesis.

Everyone talks about members, products, and revenue growth, but deposits are quietly becoming a major advantage for SoFi.

In Q1 2022, SoFi had just $1.16B in deposits.
By Q1 2026, that number reached $40.24B.

That is very impressive growth.

It gives SoFi a cheaper funding base, reduces reliance on outside funding, supports loan growth, and helps protect net interest margins over time.

The best part?
SoFi said its deposit funding created roughly $621.8M in annualized interest expense savings compared to warehouse funding.

That is a massive number.

This is why the banking side of SoFi matters so much.

The more deposits grow, the stronger the ecosystem becomes. More members, more products, more cross-buy, cheaper funding, and more long-term earnings power.

This chart shows why I continue to believe $SOFI is building something much bigger than most investors realize.

By now, it is clear that $SOFI dilution was a strategic mistake, despite it not being a financial mistake.

It's the only criticism that I see repeated again and again in comments "Noto diluted, $SOFI is a terrible company, dilution is bad".

I suspect that this dilution was actually the real reason why Muddypants attacked $SOFI. They saw that this bear narrative is strong and persistent, and wanted to cash in on it by adding gasoline to it. 

There is no objective metric by which $SOFI could be a "terrible company", yet this narrative persists because of the dilution and stock performance.

During the earnings call, they said that these note issuances were not economically dilutive to the EPS. This is because $SOFI used the $3B+ it raised to grow the EPS.

Simply put, the EPS is larger today than if they hadn't diluted, even though the share count is higher. 

Here is the real issue, investors don't care about it.

They only care about the share count going up.

It doesn't matter what Sofi does with the money, selling stock to raise funds is "bad, full stop" and the market doesn't want Sofi to do that.

If $SOFI had not done these raises, the share price today would be higher, even though the EPS would be lower.

That's why I am saying that it was a strategic mistake, despite it not being a financial mistake.

tweet.text:
$SOFI... On Trading stuff (see forwarded post) and on Take-out related stuff I'm seeing...

Market Mechanics/Trading
This isn't my deal, so I'm tagging my friends at gurula who are old school floor traders starting back in the 80s.

What would ever stop the games?
There really are only 3 attribute that stocks that are bad candidates for gaming have:

1) Buybacks
2) DVD's
3)Take out at a premium

All 3 of those things will put upward pressure on the shorts or increase the cost of doing shorting. 

Remember, the shorts are levered when they are shorting $SOFI, inherently. Even if a dvd is smaller, the dynamics change.

Should they do this 1-2?
Personally, I don't want them to do 1 or 2. With a new FED Chair coming in, they will want that capital if you get a better rate environment.

But some think rates increase. Yeah. You're going to want extra capital to have wiggle room.

What about Take Outs?
At 2x BV, far higher growth, very little real estate footprint/branches, it's not the worst candidate for at least 3 different potential buyers, though $SOFI's stated it would rather be the acquirer not the acquiree.

That said, anyone who wants to think of it needs to consider Regulation. This is Banks. Many natural obvi buyers are not allowed. 

For example: if you're like "Hey May, regulation aside, who would be a natural buyer willing to pay a premium?" 
The most obvious strategic buyer would be a foreign bank, asset manager, or  TTS (Treasury, Trust Services) provider. 

If you are large enough market cap, $SOFI gives you better tech, the US bank charter, and a start at USD stablecoin with a legit strategy to grow volume. 

That would likely get nixed immediately in the current environment.  

The only other company that might be interesting (but would also have A LOT of hurdles, structuring, or weirdness if you did it) would be Circle or Coin: of the two Circle....BUT...

But I cannot stress enough how painful that would be to get these two businesses, let alone cultures to combine, even though between the two Circle would be closer but not really....

Finally, with $C, I always suggest that b/c I don't get the impression that Jane is a permanent CEO and Noto would be a legit CEO for any big bank, the banking biz was a complete wreck (see Noto's background), and they have the TTS biz with branches that are mostly still shuttering (vs most recently increasing for all other players). 

For the non-newbies, yes, I am suggesting a reverse merger. And yes, I know it's low odds. 

But look... you can ask me or you can ask ChatGPT, Claude, or Grok. AND ALSO, whatever.... It's just a Friday opinion....🙃🙂🙃🙂🙃🙂

profile_bio.description: Believe in particular

tweet.text:
$SOFI... #fintech

Good Question posed as follows: Is Fintech (in general) more risky or is $SOFI more risky.

Here is how I think of it. 

Your only caveats are $V and $MA, but that biz model is VERY poorly understood by most people in fintech. Most do not fully realize that the mote that was created is not tech at all, but the 30 years it took them to make regulatory relationships globally.

profile_bio.description: Opinionated finance commenter mostly posting on the 400+ earnings calls I listen to a Q.  I don't give advice. It's your $, do your DD.

quoted_tweet.text:
@DeanGilles15352 @Badbod5 I can see how one might feel that way. But mostly no and this is the argument you can give anyone who might be just early days thinking about the financial sector and lending:

Bad lending, subprime lending, potentially yes. Long duration lending, also maybe, if you have someone too young who doesn't understand the concept of duration ($UPST, had literally no one with any kind of longer resume and I'm not sure if that has been corrected)

High credit rating, short duration lending? No, in general and also compared to all of emerging fintech.

The type of lending $SOFI does is very stable. fairly predictable, and that is before you layer in the strategic advantage $SOFi carries right now by being so liquid and it's specific funding and asset mix going into that part of the market.

The major macro risk to $SOFI is ONLY the following: 
A rising rate environment where you ALSO have 2 other features, 
1) an attack on illiquid assets such that private equity can no longer continue to get their returns off of liquid assets and must force sell to raise capital
2) some macro weakness that specifically attacks higher credit quality individuals. 

You need both, b/c if you only get 1 or the other, money will need to go the asset class that is the safest and that would mean a) decrease duration b) improve credit quality of assets. 

In contrast, fintech, particularly emerging fintech, is a simple porter's 5 forces analysis. Any emerging tech or new tech can create massive substitutes or new entrants that can take them out. 

And right this minute, given what's happening in AI AND ALSO, it's impact to the regulatory changes that are happening in blockchain and also banking, that is a FAR greater risk. Those changes actually help most banks/not hurt as - in general - they do less disruption and more reduction of the most expensive cost any financial firm has (labor).

Hope that clarifies.

$SOFI dropped after exceptional Q1 earnings.

Not because the business is weak — but because it didn’t raise 2026 guidance.

The market expected more.

But this is intentional.

As Anthony Noto explains, guidance at $SOFI isn’t about hype — it’s about trust.

If customers don’t trust you, they won’t give you their money.

That’s why $SOFI doesn’t push aggressive forecasts it might miss.
Missing guidance would damage the brand, reputation, and long-term credibility.

So they stay conservative.

The market wanted hype.

$SOFI chose credibility.
